text: Hello friends, this patient has cholelithiasis, cholecystitis and previous episodes of appendicitis also. So we will perform cholecystectomy and appendicitomy with the help of Mishra's knot and with the same parts.
The surgeon uses a left-hand grasper to hold the gallbladder and retract it towards the shoulder, separating minor adhesions caused by previous cholecystitis.
The duodenum is visible and is being pushed down following the separation of adhesions.
The surgeon separates the posterior peritoneum above the Rouviere's sulcus, extending the dissection to approximately one-third of the gallbladder.
The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to the Hartmann's pouch, which opens the posterior peritoneum, followed by anterolateral traction to further manipulate the gallbladder.
The surgeon opens the anterior peritoneum to create a clear posterior window, ensuring that only the peritoneum is cut.
The surgeon separates the remaining peritoneum of the cystic pedicle, completing the dissection around the gallbladder.
The surgeon separates the peritoneum over the artery, revealing the underlying structure.
The surgeon identifies and separates the peritoneum tags near the gallbladder, continuing the dissection process above the roviate sulcus.
The surgeon identifies the common bile duct (CVD) below the surgical field, highlighting its location and visibility during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects between the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring the posterior peritoneum is formed without accidentally clipping the common bile duct (CVD).
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to create an anterior window between the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring these structures are better separated.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to open its jaws and separate the peritoneum between the cystic duct and cystic artery, creating an anterior window. Small stones are visible within the cystic duct, which the surgeon milks upwards.
The surgeon visually identifies the cystic artery, cystic duct, and common bile duct (CBD) within the surgical field, highlighting their anatomical positions.

The surgeon carefully dissects above the Rouviere's sulcus, avoiding tenting of the common bile duct (CBD). The cystic duct is visible, and the peritoneum is being stripped from the cystic duct at its junction.

The surgeon completes the dissection of Calot's triangle and prepares to secure the cystic duct with an extracorporeal knot known as Misra's knot, suitable for structures up to eighteen millimeters in diameter.
That is one hitch, one wind, one lock, second wind, second lock, third wind, third lock. You can learn this knot if you will type in the Google Mishra's knot you can get the configuration.
The surgeon tightens Mishra's knot using a Vandarkar knot pusher, positioning it at the junction of the common bile duct (CBD) and the cystic duct. The knot is securely tied in place.
The surgeon applies a clip on the cystic duct, securing it above the junction with the common bile duct.
The surgeon identifies a lymph node, which is being excised for histopathological examination.
The surgeon separates the lymph node and uses stone holding forceps to extract it. A titanium clip is then applied to the cystic artery to secure it.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut between the knot and the clip, trimming the suture above the clip. The strong Mishra's knot remains intact despite attempts to pull it, demonstrating its durability before the suture is removed.

The surgeon applies entrolateral and entromedial traction to separate the gallbladder from the liver. As the dissection approaches the fundus, the surgical field is inspected for bile leaks, presence of the duct of Luschka, cystohepatic duct, or bleeding before completing the separation.

The surgeon holds the neck of the gallbladder and pulls it into the cannula, then removes the cannula. The gallbladder is cut open, and bile is suctioned out. Ovum forceps are introduced to crush and extract stones from the gallbladder, visibly reducing its size for easier extraction. The final stones are removed from the gallbladder.
The surgeon stretches the appendix and uses a harmonic scalpel to separate it from surrounding tissues.

The telescope is positioned through the epigastric port, while the harmonic device is inserted through the umbilical port. The right hypochondriac port holds the appendix, facilitating the appendectomy and cholecystectomy procedures using the same port configuration.

The skeletonization of the appendix is complete, with the appendix being held by the right hypochondriac port to provide anterior traction. The surgeon has separated the major portion of the appendix, revealing the tinea coli and reaching up to the C curve.

The surgeon uses a knot pusher to secure Mishra's knot at the base of the appendix over the cecum. The harmonic device is then brought through the right hypochondriac port to amputate the appendix, leaving a five-millimeter stump. The appendix is then held and transferred to the epigastric cannula for removal.
and now it is appendix year nine. So this was a case in which laparoscopic cholecystectomy and appendicectomy was performed with the MISRA's
